item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Going to echo some of the previous posts on this. Like the concept but it lacks details or much experimentation. I think the first thing the Foundation would do is slap a D class with a set of recording devices on there. As discussed it would almost certainly fail but that's the scientific way of doing things. You gotta fail to be able learn from those failures. No vote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmmmm. This is interesting. But I echo the other comments that something could be missing. Perhaps a few addendums (addendi??). I mean it sounds like a mini warp drive that catapults organics in an attempt to enter Venus' atmosphere all the way from another planet, which sounds cool and everything, but there's too much mystery or missing info for me to get hooked. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh. moderately interesting concept, but it feels lacking. There's just no solid hook. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, never mph, because that's imperial units. It's a bit shy of 11 million kilometers per hour; really, though, I feel that it's more important to emphasize that it's a substantial fraction of the speed of light, rather than being some vaguely super fast speed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this SCP, and found the idea of Mayans sending people to Venus as a form of sacrifice.
My one issues was with "means to a speed of approximately 0.01 c" could you possibly put that speed in mph or a measurement people are more familiar with in parenthesis.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only quibble is that the translation is indecipherable to the point of uselessness.
While it may be realistic to have such an inscription be heavily damaged and largely unreadable, it amounts to the same problem for the reader as over-redaction and adds nothing to the article.

But that's minor, I quite enjoyed it on the whole.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dig this SCP, but I'd love to see this addressed. If this thing is essentially just a catapult, pressing against the part of the subject that's touching the plate in order to accelerate them, then we should probably just end up with a splattering of gore raining down on the researchers whenever someone is launched. Instead, it seems like something more sci-fi. I'm not sure what possible alternatives there are, but… if it's like an organic magnet, repelling (or pulling, or both) all of the subject's molecules toward Venus at once, then maybe there shouldn't be a death-by-acceleration.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This isn't terrible or anything, but I'm having a hard time taking it seriously.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That "guide" is ancient, and written long before anyone had any clue what they were doing. Have you ever looked at the chain of command for a military/paramilitary unit? How about a large corporation? Four or five levels of responsibility is absurdly simple.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That guide is lame and full of butts

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Um... Moose...

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Requiring two Level 4s is always absurd: Level 4 personnel are Site Directors — two Level 3s or one Level 4 would be better.

There ain't no such specific canon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. Interesting how controversial it is, though

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is nothing wrong with getting permission from both the Site Director and the Assistant Site Director for initiating an experiment known to kill the test subjects.

The "security level" system is not comprehensive; it doesn't have nearly the same amount of granularity of actual chains of command and there's nothing that says there aren't multiple subgroups within each level.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Requiring two Level 4s is always absurd: Level 4 personnel are Site Directors — two Level 3s or one Level 4 would be better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It'd be nice if you found a way to explore the concept further, because this article does feel thin, but I like what's there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder if the ancients that built this had some way to survive the acceleration. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
omg ppl r frm venus!

That's kinda the vibe I got at least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The way I read the article, it sounded like anything attached to/with the launched subject gets launched in the same way. I imagine it almost like a bubble that's somehow protected from the effects of the launch. After all, if it's *just* the subject being launched, wouldn't things like clothes, earrings, etc. be torn off them instantaneously?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not particularly bad, and the concept is entertaining, but my interest doesn't stick to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the SCP well enough, it's got some nice implications to it.
Edit- Problem resolved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I came here to post that.

So yeah.

I'm not really digging it, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this article. It was well-written and covered an international topic that I enjoy.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Doesn't interest me, no offense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great, now that song is stuck in my head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The death-by-acceleration only occurs if the acceleration is being imparted unevenly into the material. After all, what breaks you apart when you accelerate/decelerate is that your different bits don't all achieve the final speed at the same time.

So, assuming the human doesn't die instantly on acceleration nor tears out of their own clothes, either have them hold a camera close enough that it counts as part of them, or put it in their mouths.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's precedent for at least some of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Lemme guess.
Venus was once a perfectly habitable planet.
Then our ancient human ancestors who lived there messed it up.
So they decided to try another planet. They adapted to it and figured it out but the Mayans kept a thing around that would let them go "back home".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It just… lauches people at Venus, along with something about returning humans to Venus (?), and I'm not really drawn in by it. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So you might say, that on venus…

it's raining men?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know. Nothing really grabs me. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hate playing Mass Effect; now, all I can think is "sooo…. It's a Relay point calibrated to move humans to Venus…" Doesn't particularly have anything to make me feel any emotion about it, either… -1 for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This… just, yes. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ach, yeah, ignore me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two problems:

Accelerating from 0 to 0.01c, or 1% of the speed of light, is an increase of 10,792,528 kilometers per hour. Have you ever tried to catch a flying bowling ball? Assuming you have someone holding a 5kg probe, that probe will suddenly feel like it weighs like neutronium. The best case scenario, it snaps all your fingers. Worst case scenario, you are now unarmed.

Now, remember relativity. Going from zero to 0.01c is effectively the same acceleration as going from 0.01c to zero. That means that the probe itself, even if you could hold onto it, just underwent the same kinetic force as if it had impacted a solid surface at 1% the speed of light. Your average camera won't even survive being dropped from a hot air balloon, much less while going at relativistic speed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You only need the D-Class to hold onto the probe long enough to launch it, unless I am completely misinterpreting how this thing works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a man cannon! Just imagine if it only shot women up…

I like it for the simple matter in which it's presented.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's what they're trying to do. So far nothing has survived acceleration from zero to 0.01c in under a second.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hrm. And they don't try to send someone with a probe? Maybe see what's on the other side?

It's nice enough, but lacking in meat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Risking sounding like a jackass (well, more than usual), I'm going to go with my gut and downvote this. There really isn't a concrete reason as to why (writing is serviceable, idea is inoffensive) other than the general 'ehh' I felt while reading this.
